critic_raw:
This is a well-designed experiment with genuine attention to leakage prevention, confound control, and metric justification. The temporal split is correct. The equal-tuning-effort baseline is appropriate. The bootstrap CIs on the test set are appropriate. That said, there are three substantive methodological issues worth raising, organized by their root assumptions.

---

**Issue 1: The test-set 'confirmation pass' before final retraining constitutes test-set-informed model selection**

The claim being made: Section 8 describes a 'single confirmatory pass' on months 31–36 to verify the chosen configuration before final retraining. The intent is to guard against a configuration that is overly tuned to the validation window.

Why this might be wrong: This confirmation step is, by definition, a use of test-set information to make a modeling decision. Even if the decision is binary ('confirm or revert'), the act of conditioning retraining on test-set performance introduces selection bias. If the configuration fails on months 31–36, the design presumably requires going back — selecting an alternate configuration — which means the test set has now influenced which model gets reported. Even if the protocol forbids going back, the act of 'verifying the advantage holds' means the reported test-set evaluation is not blind: the analyst knows whether the configuration 'worked' before the final number is written down. This is a form of multi-stage testing without correction, and it inflates reported accuracy.

What would constitute evidence: A design that separates this concern correctly would either (a) use a third held-out window for confirmation (months 31–33) and reserve months 34–36 as the true blind test, (b) report validation-set performance as the primary comparison metric with the test set used only once, or (c) pre-register the confirmation rule before touching months 31–36 and report both the validation and test numbers unconditionally regardless of alignment. If the confirmation step has never caused a rollback in practice, it is vacuous; if it sometimes causes rollback, it is leaking.

---

**Issue 2: Excluding reassigned tickets biases the training label distribution toward easy cases**

The claim being made: The design excludes tickets where reassignment occurred during resolution to avoid label ambiguity. The target label is the final resolving team.

Why this might be wrong: Reassigned tickets are precisely the ambiguous cases — tickets that were initially routed incorrectly or that genuinely span multiple queue responsibilities. Excluding them means the training set is composed disproportionately of tickets where the initial routing was unambiguous or correct. The model will be trained on 'clean' routing decisions. But in production, the model must also handle the hard cases — the ones that get reassigned — because it will encounter them in the live queue. The reported accuracy on the held-out test set also excludes reassigned tickets (the split is applied uniformly), so the evaluation metric is systematically optimistic about the scenario the model is actually deployed into. The 'faster first-response time' claim is particularly affected: if the model is only evaluated on tickets that historically needed no reassignment, the FRT improvement claim may not generalize to the ticket population where reassignment (and delay) actually occurs.

What would constitute evidence: The proportion of reassigned tickets in the 36-month corpus should be reported. If it is below 5%, the exclusion is defensible. If it is 15–30% (common in real support systems), the exclusion invalidates the FRT claim and overstates routing accuracy. A secondary evaluation on reassigned tickets — even without clean ground-truth labels — using proxy metrics like time-to-resolution or final queue as a noisy label, would bound the optimism.

---

**Issue 3: Including agent reply history in transformer inputs but not controlling for information-time ordering creates implicit leakage**

The claim being made: The transformer input concatenates 'subject + body + reply history.' The preprocessing applies tokenizers fit only on training data, so there is no vocabulary leakage. The temporal split prevents future tickets from appearing in training.

Why this might be wrong: Agent reply history, at inference time, is only available after an agent has already interacted with the ticket. If reply history includes the agent's own responses (not just customer follow-ups), it may contain routing-relevant signals generated after the initial triage decision — for example, an agent writing 'I'm escalating this to the billing team' or referencing a product by name in a way that reveals the destination queue. During training, the model can learn to predict the resolving team from signals that only appear in the reply thread after routing has already occurred. At actual deployment time (first-contact routing), the reply history does not yet exist. The model as trained may be solving a partially retrospective problem, not a real-time routing problem.

What would constitute evidence: An ablation that evaluates the transformer trained on subject+body only versus subject+body+reply_history would directly measure how much of the performance gain comes from reply content. If the gap is large, the design is solving a different (easier) problem than the one the deployment scenario requires. The section on 'faster first-response time' implies the model is used at ticket creation — if so, reply history is unavailable and this feature is architecturally incompatible with the stated deployment scenario.
all_issues_raised: The 'confirmatory pass' on months 31–36 before final retraining is a form of test-set-informed model selection that inflates reported accuracy even if only used as a binary check.

Excluding reassigned tickets from both training and evaluation biases the label distribution toward easy cases and makes the first-response-time improvement claim non-generalizable to the ambiguous tickets where reassignment (and delay) actually occurs.

Including agent reply history in transformer inputs potentially leaks post-routing information that would not be available at first-contact triage time, making the model solve a retrospective problem rather than the real-time routing problem the deployment scenario requires.
The 'confirmatory pass' on the test set before final retraining is a form of adaptive test-set reuse that invalidates the held-out guarantee and biases reported metrics upward.
Final retraining on months 1–30 uses hyperparameters tuned on the months 1–24 training regime, creating a mismatched configuration that is never validated at the larger training set size.

Excluding reassigned tickets removes the hardest, most ambiguous routing cases from both training and evaluation, potentially overstating the transformer's advantage over the baseline on production-realistic ticket difficulty.

The 'confirmatory' test-set pass is a soft selection step: if the config doesn't confirm, the design implicitly allows re-evaluation, making the reported result conditional on test-set confirmation and therefore selected rather than verified.
Hyperparameters are tuned for the 24-month training regime but applied to the 30-month retraining, introducing a size-mismatch that may differentially affect the transformer vs. XGBoost.
Agent reply history may encode routing decisions or team-specific language, creating label leakage that the transformer can exploit more effectively than TF-IDF, inflating its apparent advantage.

Weighted F1 as the primary tuning and selection metric steers both models toward high-volume queue performance, potentially at the expense of specialist queues that may be the core operational motivation.

issues_raised: The 'confirmatory pass' on the test set before final retraining is a form of adaptive test-set reuse that invalidates the held-out guarantee and biases reported metrics upward.
Final retraining on months 1–30 uses hyperparameters tuned on the months 1–24 training regime, creating a mismatched configuration that is never validated at the larger training set size.

Excluding reassigned tickets removes the hardest, most ambiguous routing cases from both training and evaluation, potentially overstating the transformer's advantage over the baseline on production-realistic ticket difficulty.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a generally well-designed experiment. The temporal split is correctly motivated, feature parity between models is addressed, and the scope is appropriately bounded. However, I identify three genuine methodological issues, not implementation nits.

**Issue 1: Test-set peeking via the 'confirmatory pass' invalidates the held-out guarantee**

The specific claim: Section 8 describes selecting the best hyperparameter configuration on the validation set and then performing 'a single confirmatory pass on the test window' to verify the configuration maintains its advantage before final retraining. The framing treats this as a guard against validation-period overfitting.

Why this is wrong: The moment test-set labels are consulted to decide whether to accept or reject a configuration, the test set is no longer held out — it has become a second validation criterion. Even a single binary decision (accept/reject) based on test-set performance constitutes a form of selection pressure. If the confirmatory pass reveals that the chosen configuration does not 'maintain its advantage' on months 31–36, the design implies iterating or reconsidering; otherwise, why perform the check at all? The result is that the reported test-set metrics reflect a configuration that survived a test-set gate, inflating the probability that observed performance is a statistical artifact of that gate rather than genuine generalization. This is exactly the same structure as adaptive test-set reuse, just with one additional selection step. The confidence intervals computed afterward are not valid under this protocol.

Evidence: If the confirmatory pass never changes the selected configuration (i.e., the validation winner always passes), the step is vacuous and should be removed. If it sometimes changes the selection, then the test-set contamination is direct and reported metrics are optimistically biased. The design needs to choose: either use the test set exactly once for reporting, or introduce a true third holdout window (months 31–36 remains sealed; a months 25–30 validation serves as the gate). The current hybrid cannot be rescued by calling it 'confirmatory.'

**Issue 2: Final retraining on months 1–30 with hyperparameters tuned on months 25–30 creates a distribution mismatch that is not evaluated**

The specific claim: After hyperparameter selection, the model is retrained on the combined training + validation set (months 1–30) to maximize training signal. The selected hyperparameters — including early stopping patience, learning rate, batch size — were chosen based on the validation loss trajectory observed when training on months 1–24 only.

Why this is wrong: Hyperparameters such as number of epochs, early stopping patience, and learning rate schedule are implicitly calibrated to the size and distribution of the months 1–24 training set. When the training set is expanded to months 1–30 (a 25% larger dataset with a different temporal distribution), the optimal hyperparameters may differ. In particular: the number of epochs before overfitting typically changes with dataset size; the learning rate that converges well on 24 months of data may over- or under-shoot on 30 months; and early stopping cannot operate in the same way because there is no validation signal during final retraining. The experiment reports test-set metrics from this retrained model, but its hyperparameters were never validated at this training set size. This is a common but real methodological gap.

Evidence: Compare test-set performance of the model trained on months 1–24 (with hyperparameters correctly matched to that training regime) against the model retrained on months 1–30 with the same hyperparameters. If the retrained model performs worse, the combined-data retraining step is actively harmful. The experiment currently reports only the retrained model's metrics and has no way to detect this failure mode.

**Issue 3: Exclusion of reassigned tickets biases both the label distribution and the difficulty distribution in ways that favor the transformer**

The specific claim: Only resolved tickets with no reassignments are included. The justification is label ambiguity avoidance.

Why this is wrong: Reassigned tickets are precisely the ambiguous, difficult-to-route cases — the ones where human agents themselves disagreed about the correct queue. Excluding them removes the hardest examples from the evaluation, making the routing problem easier than production reality. Worse, the transformer may disproportionately benefit from this exclusion: pre-trained language models tend to perform well on clear, well-formed tickets but degrade on the noisy, edge-case tickets that trigger reassignment. The TF-IDF baseline's relative performance on ambiguous tickets (which may rely less on semantic coherence) is never tested. The reported accuracy gap between transformer and baseline on clean-label tickets may not replicate on the full production distribution, which includes reassigned cases.

Evidence: Compute per-model accuracy separately on (a) tickets with no reassignment and (b) tickets that were eventually resolved after reassignment (using the final resolving team as the label, accepting the ambiguity). If the transformer's advantage shrinks or reverses on reassigned tickets, the deployment performance claim is overstated. If the gap is consistent, the exclusion is benign. The current design has no way to distinguish these cases.

**Summary:** Issue 1 is a structural validity problem that invalidates the held-out test-set guarantee. Issues 2 and 3 introduce optimistic bias in the reported performance figures. None of these are resolvable by analysis alone — each requires a design change or supplementary empirical check.

issues_raised: The 'confirmatory' test-set pass is a soft selection step: if the config doesn't confirm, the design implicitly allows re-evaluation, making the reported result conditional on test-set confirmation and therefore selected rather than verified.
Hyperparameters are tuned for the 24-month training regime but applied to the 30-month retraining, introducing a size-mismatch that may differentially affect the transformer vs. XGBoost.
Agent reply history may encode routing decisions or team-specific language, creating label leakage that the transformer can exploit more effectively than TF-IDF, inflating its apparent advantage.

Weighted F1 as the primary tuning and selection metric steers both models toward high-volume queue performance, potentially at the expense of specialist queues that may be the core operational motivation.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a well-structured experimental design. The temporal split, equal tuning effort, preprocessing discipline (fit-on-train-only), and explicit confound controls are all legitimate and thoughtful. I will focus on genuine structural flaws rather than nits.

### Issue 1: The confirmatory test-set pass creates a selection step, not just a confirmation step

**Claim:** The single pass on the test set (months 31–36) before final retraining is a 'confirmatory' step that guards against validation-period overfit, not a hyperparameter selection step.

**Why it might be wrong:** The design says: identify the best config on validation, then check it holds on the test window, and if confirmed, retrain and report on that same test window. But if the check reveals the config *doesn't* hold, what happens? The design doesn't say. The implicit logic is: if validation-period config doesn't transfer, you'd presumably go back and pick differently. That makes the test set a *selection criterion*, not just a verification criterion — regardless of how many configs are evaluated against it. Even a single conditional look that could trigger a redo contaminates the test set. This inflates apparent generalization because the reported result conditions on the test set having confirmed the config. The mechanism is selection bias: you only report results from test windows where the validation config happened to transfer; cases where it didn't would presumably trigger re-evaluation.

**Evidence:** If the design were truly confirmatory, the protocol would commit to reporting results regardless of whether confirmation succeeded or failed, with the confirmatory check's outcome itself reported as a secondary finding. The absence of a pre-committed fallback reveals the step's true nature as soft selection.

---

### Issue 2: Retraining on months 1–30 after selecting hyperparameters on months 25–30 (a subset) introduces a mismatch that the design treats as neutral

**Claim:** The final model is retrained on months 1–30 using the hyperparameters selected by performance on months 25–30 alone; this 'maximizes training signal.'

**Why it might be wrong:** Hyperparameters tuned for the data distribution of months 1–24 predicting months 25–30 may not be optimal for months 1–30 predicting months 31–36. Specifically: the optimal regularization, number of epochs, and early stopping point depend on training-set size. DistilBERT and XGBoost both have hyperparameters (learning rate schedule, max depth, subsample) sensitive to dataset size. A larger training set (30 months vs. 24 months) typically benefits from different regularization than a smaller one. The selected hyperparameters are biased toward the smaller training regime. This isn't a catastrophic flaw, but it means the final reported performance may understate what either model would achieve with properly tuned hyperparameters for the full 1–30 window — which could bias the comparison if the transformer and baseline respond differently to this mismatch (e.g., transformers are typically more sensitive to learning rate schedules than XGBoost).

**Evidence:** Run a secondary experiment where hyperparameters are also searched on the full 1–30 window using a held-out slice (e.g., months 28–30) as a mini-validation. If the results shift materially, the mismatch is load-bearing. If not, the concern is minor.

---

### Issue 3: Reply history in transformer input but not cleanly controlled as a leakage vector

**Claim:** Agent reply history is a valid input feature that both models receive equally; including it allows the transformer to use richer context.

**Why it might be wrong:** Agent replies often contain routing decisions themselves — e.g., 'I'm transferring you to the billing team' or template phrases that are team-specific. If reply history is included in the input and the label is the final resolving team, the model may be learning to read explicit routing metadata embedded in agent language rather than learning to route from the ticket content. This is a form of label leakage: the reply history is generated *after* routing has already occurred and may directly encode the routing label. The confound is asymmetric: the transformer, with its ability to attend to specific phrases over long sequences, is better positioned to exploit this signal than TF-IDF, meaning any observed advantage could reflect leakage exploitation rather than semantic routing ability.

The design notes that 'reassignments during resolution are excluded' to avoid label ambiguity — but it does not exclude agent reply content from the feature set, which may encode the resolved-team identity.

**Evidence:** Ablate reply history from both models. If transformer advantage drops substantially when reply history is removed, the advantage was at least partly driven by this signal. Compare per-ticket accuracy against whether reply history mentions team names or routing-specific language.

---

### Issue 4: Weighted F1 as primary metric may mask the operationally relevant failure mode

**Claim:** Weighted F1 reflects 'how often the model is correct under the actual queue volume encountered in production' and is appropriate because the routing system will operate under the live ticket mix.

**Why it might be wrong:** In a support routing system, the cost of misrouting is asymmetric across queues — misrouting a ticket to a high-volume queue (which handles 30–40% of traffic) is likely much cheaper than misrouting to a specialist queue (which handles <5%), because the high-volume queue has more agents to absorb and redirect. Weighted F1 weights by class frequency, which means the metric is dominated by the queues that are cheapest to misroute *to*, while systematically discounting performance on the specialist queues that may be the primary motivation for the routing system in the first place. If the experiment's business case is about getting specialist-queue tickets to the right team quickly, weighted F1 will not surface whether that goal is achieved. The design includes macro-F1 as a secondary metric, which is an improvement, but the primary metric governing model selection (hyperparameter tuning is by weighted F1) will steer both models away from specialist-queue precision.

This is not just a reporting issue — it affects which model is selected as 'better,' because a model that excels on high-volume queues at the expense of specialist queues will win on weighted F1 but may fail the operational objective.

**Evidence:** Report the model selection decision under macro-F1 and per-queue F1 as primary criteria and check whether it agrees with the weighted-F1-based selection. A disagreement reveals that the metric choice is doing real work in determining the winner.

---

## Summary

Issues 1 and 3 are genuine structural flaws that could invalidate specific conclusions: Issue 1 makes the test-set evaluation conditionally contaminated; Issue 3 could explain transformer superiority as leakage rather than capability. Issues 2 and 4 are real but milder — they introduce bias rather than invalidating the experiment outright. The design is otherwise sophisticated and above average for applied ML work.
